sop.coo_evaluation:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member complaints & incident logging** – no documented escalation path for injuries, billing disputes, harassment, or facility issues; critical for liability and pattern-spotting
- **Win-back/reactivation campaign** – no SOP for identifying lapsed members, outreach sequencing, or re-onboarding; retention is cheaper than acquisition
- **Coach performance management & feedback cycles** – no documented review schedule, capability conversations, or capability plan process
- **Financial reconciliation & cash handling** – no SOP for daily/weekly cash counts, discrepancy resolution, or audit trail if you're handling card/cash payments
- **Health & safety incident response** – no documented steps for injuries on-site, near-misses, emergency contact protocols, or incident reporting to insurance
- **Member data breach/privacy incident response** – GDPR-relevant; no SOP for handling member data security issues
- **Complaints resolution & appeals** – no formal process for member grievances, timelines for response, or escalation to management

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Client onboarding** – likely missing: fitness assessment documentation standards, liability waiver + photo consent capture workflow, first-session coach assignment logic, and follow-up cadence at day 3, 7, 14
- **Coach induction** – check if it covers: studio brand voice/tone for Instagram/TikTok coaching content, how to handle difficult member conversations, and badge/credential verification
- **Marketing** – Email Newsletter & Blog Post are flagged as missing; also audit existing 10 SOPs for: lead magnet delivery workflow, member referral incentive redemption, and social media crisis response
- **Facilities** – 4 SOPs may not cover: deep clean/monthly maintenance schedule, equipment breakage reporting & repair SLA, or seasonal closure/reopening checklists
- **

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO BRIEF

## REVENUE — CRITICAL ISSUE

**June vs May: Revenue down 12.3% (£20,199 → £17,711) despite member base up 20.5% (122 → 147). Your ARPU collapsed £165.56 → £120.48. This is your biggest leak.**

- **Fix pricing immediately.** You've added 25 members but lost £2,488 revenue. Either your new members joined at discount rates or your class/package mix shifted downward. Audit June's new joiner deals vs May. Raise entry-level package prices by 8–10% (test on new sign-ups first) and re-tier your offerings to push more members toward premium tiers (PT add-ons, unlimited+ plans, boot camp bundles).
- **Churn collapsed to 0% in June but ARPU tells the real story.** You're signing people cheap. Review your June acquisition cost and cohort quality—if these are trial members or discounted onboards, tighten terms. Aim for £165+ ARPU by end of July.
- **Activate the 147-member base for upsells.** With 25 new members, you have a 3-month engagement window. Push PT consultations, nutrition plans, and class packages (Block/Unlimited upgrade) in month 2–3 of membership. June's non-people costs (£9,390) suggest possible PT/PT kit spend—are you investing in PT capacity but not selling it?

---

## EXPENSES — IMMEDIATE SCRUTINY ON NON-PEOPLE COSTS

**Q2 (May–June) vs Q1 (Feb–Mar): Revenue flat (~£17–20k), but non-people costs shot up 12.6% (£9,162 avg → £9,312 avg). Post-April, you should see expenses scale *down* as a % of revenue, not stay bloated.**

- **Non-people expenses in June at £9,390 (53% of revenue) are unsustainable.** May was better (46%), so June was an outlier—audit it. Isolate variable vs fixed: utilities, supplies, cleaning, PT commissions, insurance, landlord costs on the new studio. The move bought space to *grow* not to *spend more*. If rent/utilities jumped post-relocation, model the breakeven point (you need ~180 active members at current ARPU to comfortably absorb the bigger footprint).
- **Payroll trending up (£3,550 May → £3,686 June).** With member

staff.advice:
• **JoJo is significantly overloaded at 133 sessions.** She's carrying 56% of the team's workload and is the sole coach for Circuits (6 sessions) and dominant on Build: Upper (15/33 sessions). Redistribute some Build: Upper and Circuits classes to Eilis or consider adding a fourth coach if demand is sustainable.

• **Grace's Monday-only constraint leaves significant gaps mid-week.** Build: Lower (17 sessions, all Grace) is entirely dependent on one coach across the month. Assign Eilis to cover some Build: Lower on Wed/Thu/Fri to create resilience and free Grace's content focus for scaling.

• **Eilis is under-utilised and ready for growth.** At 48 sessions, she has capacity. She's untested on Circuits and Build: Upper — these would stretch her skills safely beyond her current Elevate/Build: Full Body mix. Start with one Circuits or Build: Upper class per week to build confidence.

• **Body Composition Scans are fragmented across all three coaches (40 sessions total).** Consolidate these onto one primary coach (suggest JoJo) with Eilis as backup. This reduces context-switching and frees Grace and Eilis for class-led work.

• **Personal Training (55 sessions) leans heavily on JoJo (44).** Shift 8–10 1:1 slots to Eilis monthly to balance her timetable and reduce JoJo's load; this suits Eilis's detail-oriented style.

• **No Sweat Intros (3 sessions, Grace only) should rotate to Eilis or a structured rota.** Quick win to free Grace for strategic work and give Eilis consistent member touchpoints.

class_stats.suggestion:
**For quiet studio time gaps:**

1. **Add a paid 30-min "Open Lift" block on Monday/Thursday mornings** — your quietest days have dead air between PT sessions; charge £8-12 for members who want structured barbell time without a full class commitment, targeting the Build-class loyalists who might squeeze in extra volume.

2. **Run a monthly "Intro to Engine" workshop (45 mins, Saturday mornings)** — Engine is severely underattended (15-52 across periods); a one-off, low-pressure intro session removes the intimidation barrier and converts curious members who've never tried conditioning-focused work.

**For the struggling classes themselves:**

3. **Move Barbelle and Strongevity to Wednesday 6-6:45pm, right after Build: Upper finishes** — both classes have collapsed (12-13 attendance); your Wednesday peak means members are already in the gym; offer the first 3 sessions free to Build: Upper finishers as a "try a new class" incentive, bundling them as a natural progression rather than standalone offerings.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Execution for The Forge

• **Start with your profit ladder, not alphabetical lists.** Map each offering by margin: tier your class passes (drop-in £8 → 10-pack £65 → unlimited £49/month), then bolt on high-margin add-ons (PT sessions, nutrition plans, challenges). This forces you to price strategically, not guess. Do this in a spreadsheet today—revenue potential becomes obvious.

• **Build two separate menus—one kills coaches, one sells members.** Coaches need: what's included in their shift (class capacity, equipment setup, music rights), what they can upsell (1-to-1, form checks), commission structure. Members see: benefit-focused descriptions ("strength building 45-min class"), price, what's truly included (locker access? towel service?). Design these side-by-side so you spot conflicts immediately.

• **Nail the "what's included" language NOW—it stops refund arguments later.** Instead of vague ("access to all classes"), write: "Unlimited class access, Mon-Fri 6am-8pm, excludes bootcamp intensives. Locker access only during session time." Ambiguity costs you complaints and chargebacks; specificity = fewer friction points with both coaches and members.

• **Publish internally first, collect 2 weeks of coach feedback.** Coaches will spot pricing/logistics issues you missed. Make revisions, then soft-launch to members via WhatsApp/email—don't go public until you've stress-tested it with real questions. This catches problems before they damage trust.

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement — Execution Blueprint for Grace

• **Start with exit data, not assumptions.** Pull your analytics right now — which pages have high bounce rates? Where do visitors drop off? Talk to 5-10 members who *almost* joined but didn't. Ask: "What made you hesitate?" This reveals the actual confusion points, not what you *think* is unclear.

• **Build a "Objection Map" section on your homepage.** Women are researching women-only gyms for specific reasons (safety, no judgment, different vibe). Answer directly: *"Is this for beginners only?"* *"What if I'm intimidated?"* *"Do I need experience?"* Put these as FAQ-style blocks above the fold with short, confident answers.

• **Clarity audit: One sentence per page.** Re-read every landing page and write down what The Forge *actually* offers in 15 words. If you can't, your copy can't either. Example: "Strength training and classes designed for women, by women — no judgment, all levels." Use that as your North Star for rewrites.

• **A/B test the signup CTA immediately.** Change button text from generic "Join Now" to specific action: *"Start Free Trial"* or *"Book Your First Session."* Test on 3-4 pages this week. Specificity removes ambiguity and increases conversions.

• **Audit competitor landing pages (not to copy, but to benchmark).** Look at 3-4 other women-focused gyms. What questions do *they* answer? Where do they differ? This shows you what questions women are actually asking the market.

**Action this week:** Grab

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Internal Feedback System — Execute This Way

• **Start with post-class check-ins, not forms.** Have instructors ask 2 questions while members cool down: "What worked for you today?" and "What would make this better?" Takes 60 seconds, captures honest feedback when endorphins are high, costs nothing. Forms get ignored—conversations drive action.

• **Pick ONE metric to track religiously.** Don't ask 10 questions. Monthly, measure: "Would you recommend The Forge to a friend?" (Net Promoter Score). It predicts retention and identifies who's about to churn. Tag responses by class/instructor so you spot patterns (e.g., "spinning class dropping off").

• **Close the loop visibly.** When feedback leads to change—new equipment, adjusted class times, music playlist tweak—tell members what you did because of *their* input. Post it in the gym or mention it in welcome announcements. This makes feedback feel powerful, not performative, and people give better suggestions next time.

• **Make instructors accountable for insights, not just collection.** Monthly 10-minute huddle: each instructor shares the 1-2 themes they heard from their classes and proposes one small fix. Ownership drives better feedback quality and faster implementation.

• **Use feedback to drive referrals.** Members who feel heard refer more. When someone gives constructive input and sees it acted on, ask: "Who else would love this kind of gym?"

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Strategy for The Forge

• **Start with your REVENUE tier, not alphabetically** — Personal Training members drive highest lifetime value, so write that SOP first. You'll learn your documentation style and can apply it faster to lower-revenue streams. Knock it out in 2-3 days max, then use it as your template.

• **Interview your actual staff member who handles each service** — Don't write from memory. Sit with whoever onboards PT clients or processes Fuel Forward orders for 30 mins. They know the friction points and workarounds you've forgotten. This takes 2 hours but saves rewriting later.

• **Create ONE-PAGE SOPs, not manuals** — For each service, use: What members expect on Day 1 → What staff does → Common problems + fixes. Anything longer won't get read by staff or used consistently. Google Docs template, shared folder, done.

• **Embed the SOP in your actual workflow immediately** — Don't file it away. Link it in your booking system (if digital), print it at the front desk, or share in staff Slack channel. If staff aren't using it within 48 hours of writing, it's already dead.

• **Build 1 quick feedback loop** — After 2 weeks, ask staff: "What's missing or wrong in the PT/Fuel Forward SOP?" One 10-minute chat catches 80% of gaps before you've documented all five services.

**Timeline: 3 weeks to launch all 5 if you start Monday.**

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Calendar Execution for The Forge

• **Use a free 8-week template TODAY** — Don't build from scratch. Grab a pre-made fitness gym calendar (Canva, Buffer, or Notion templates exist), customize the 20% that's specific to The Forge (member wins, class schedules, local events), and you're live. Thursday = planning day for next 2 weeks. Takes 90 minutes max per cycle.

• **Create a 5-post content formula that repeats** — Rotate: (1) Member transformation/testimonial, (2) Class highlight with real footage, (3) Motivation + stats (e.g., "members averaged X strength gains"), (4) Behind-the-scenes/team story, (5) Local community angle or partner shout-out. This removes the "what do I post?" paralysis. You're mixing, not inventing.

• **Assign ONE person (you or a staff member) as the "feeder"** — They spend 15 mins daily capturing raw content: quick phone videos of packed classes, member quotes after sessions, facility updates. Dump these in a shared folder. Your content planner cherry-picks from this stockpile rather than starting blank. Removes the bottleneck.

• **Batch-create monthly on a specific date** — First Monday of each month, spend 2 hours scheduling 12-16 posts at once using Buffer or Meta's native scheduler. You're not scrambling Wednesday night. Posts go live automatically; you just monitor comments.

• **Track ONE metric ruthlessly** — Which post type drives the most profile visits or gym inquiries? That's your North Star. Double down on it. If member testimonials drive

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Execute Videography Sessions with Jordan - Concrete Actions

**1. Lock it in NOW with a standing booking**
Ring Jordan today. Book the next 4 sessions (8, 16, 24, 32 weeks out) as recurring calendar blocks. Get it in writing via email so it's non-negotiable. This beats scheduling ad-hoc—you'll actually do it.

**2. Create a 48-hour pre-session content brief**
Two days before each shoot, send Jordan a 1-page list: 3-4 specific "hero" shots you need (e.g., "new member first day moment," "class highlight," "transformation testimonial"). Don't wing it. This turns random footage into usable assets.

**3. Assign one staff member as "on-set producer"**
Pick someone to wrangle members, coordinate timing, and ensure 2-3 quick testimonial clips are captured while Jordan shoots. You'll get 3x the usable content without slowing operations.

**4. Pre-film talking points with your phone**
The week before, record yourself on your phone (60 seconds each) with 2-3 things you want to say about The Forge that month. Hand these clips to Jordan—he can weave them into the edit and you skip the "camera panic" moment.

**5. Repurpose immediately into a content calendar**
The day you receive final video, chop it into: 1 Reel, 3 TikToks, 2 LinkedIn posts, 1 email thumbnail. Batch this task—don't let footage sit unused.

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Systemize Automations for 6-Week Trial Enquiries

**1. Map the actual breakdown first**
Before touching any tool, document what happens NOW when a trial enquiry lands: Who responds? When? What do they say? How many drop off between enquiry and first session? This audit takes 2 hours max but reveals where automation actually saves time vs. where personal touch matters (hint: it's not where you think).

**2. Build a 3-email sequence, not a system**
Day 0 (instant): Confirmation + gym tour video link + "here's what to expect" 
Day 2: Gentle nudge with member success story from similar background
Day 5: Final reminder + offer (extend trial if they no-showed, or lock in member discount if converting)
Set these in Mailchimp or Typeform (not Zapier overkill). Test with 20 people manually first.

**3. Automate the lead qualification gate, not the welcome**
Add 2-3 quick questions to your enquiry form ("What's your fitness goal? Available times?"). Conditional logic routes hot leads (evenings + strength training = your core demo) to Grace's inbox same-day; lukewarm ones into the email sequence. This filters signal from noise immediately.

**4. Track the ONE metric that matters**
Trial-to-membership conversion rate. Set baseline this week, retest in 6 weeks. If it's 60%+ now, automation might actually kill conversion by removing Grace's personal edge—focus instead on consistency. If it's <40%, automation buys you time to fix the actual conversion problem (bad induction? pricing?).

**5. Own the hand

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Execute Role-Specific Action Checklists for The Forge

**1. Start with outcome backwards, then build checklists around it**
Don't create generic task lists. For each person, ask: "What does success look like weekly?" (JoJo might own member retention metrics, Eilis member acquisition, Sarah revenue/operations). Write the **KPI first**, then list only the 5-7 actions that directly move that needle. Anything that doesn't touch the KPI gets cut.

**2. Make checklists daily-actionable, not weekly-vague**
Instead of "increase member retention," JoJo's checklist should read: "Monday: check member attendance dips >2 weeks, Tuesday: contact 3 at-risk members, Wednesday: report churn rate." Time-specific actions prevent procrastination and make accountability automatic.

**3. Build in a 15-min Friday sync ritual**
Each person submits their KPI performance (one number per role) every Friday at 4pm to you. No long reports—just the metric. This forces them to track weekly, not scramble monthly, and gives you real-time visibility to course-correct fast.

**4. Document in a shared, live spreadsheet they update daily**
Not a static PDF. Create one sheet per person with their 3 KPIs + weekly actions, and they tick off each action as done. You can spot patterns (What's blocking Eilis?) without micromanaging. Share read-only view with the team so everyone sees what others own.

**5. Test it for 2 weeks, then refine**
Roll this out with one person first (probably JoJo), nail the format, then

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# How Grace Should Run This Vision Session

• **Pre-session prep (do this before people arrive):** Send a 1-pager 48 hours prior with the SSO, 90-day milestones, and a rough draft of role lanes. This stops the session being discovery work—people arrive primed and you control the narrative from minute one.

• **Open with the *why* they should care:** Lead with revenue/member growth targets tied directly to their role outcomes (e.g., "If retention hits 85%, that's £X extra"). Make it personal. Abstract mission statements die in gyms; money and impact stick.

• **Walk through lanes role-by-role, not by function:** Don't say "marketing owns acquisition." Say: "Sarah, you're running the referral program pilot + Instagram content calendar. Here's what success looks like by day 45." Get explicit commitments, not vague nodding.

• **Surface one constraint per person, right then:** Ask each team member: "What one thing will block your lane in the next 90 days?" Capture it live. This prevents excuses later—they've already flagged it, so you own removing blockers together.

• **End with a written recap they sign off on (digitally same day):** Each person gets their lane + 3 key metrics + one decision they own. Send it within 2 hours. No ambiguity about what they agreed to.

**Why this works:** Most team sessions are feel-good theater. This is a contract.

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Audit & Rebalance: The Forge Team Workload (30 Days)

**Week 1 — Surface the Real Picture**
- Have Grace shadow each team member for 2-3 hours (not observe from office—actually follow them through their day). Note every task, interruption, admin work. This beats surveys; you'll spot the bottlenecks causing stress.
- Ask each person directly: "What 3 tasks would you drop tomorrow if you could?" This reveals what's actually crushing them vs. what they think should matter.

**Week 2 — Map to Revenue Impact**
- Separate tasks into tiers: *drives member retention/revenue* (classes, member check-ins, sales conversations) vs. *necessary but draining* (scheduling, cleaning rotas, emails). Over-stretched staff are usually drowning in Tier 2 while Tier 1 suffers.
- Assign Tier 2 admin to whoever has capacity—or batch it (e.g., all scheduling done Mondays 9-11am by one person, not scattered).

**Weeks 3-4 — Rebalance & Test**
- Shift 2-3 specific tasks per person. Make it *visible*: "Sarah, you're dropping email management Tuesday-Thursday. James covers it." Not vague.
- Check in mid-week to catch friction early (personality clashes, skill gaps). Adjust immediately—don't wait 30 days.

**The Real Win**
Track one metric per person: classes taught, new member consultations, or member retention rate. If rebalancing works, their primary output should improve within 30 days. That's your proof it's working

# How Grace Cuts 10% Without Gutting Operations

• **Energy audit first** — Walk the gym floor with phone timer. Track which machines run when/how often, lighting zones, heating patterns. Most gyms hemorrhage 15-20% on utilities. A quick fix: motion sensors in changing rooms, LED retrofit on high-usage areas. That's 3-4% right there, minimal capex.

• **Supplier renegotiation, not replacement** — Call your top 3 vendors (cleaning, equipment maintenance, towel service) and tell them you're getting competitive quotes. You probably aren't, but they don't know. Ask for 8-12% discounts. You'll land 5-7% from at least one. Takes 3 calls, saves real money immediately.

• **Staffing model audit** — Map actual foot traffic by hour. You're likely overstaffed in dead zones (11am-2pm?) and understaffed in peaks. Shift one person's hours, reduce one part-timer's contract by 4 hours/week strategically. Don't cut headcount—redistribute it. Saves payroll without killing member experience.

• **Membership holder intel** — Pull your data: which members use the gym <2x/month? Hit them with a "we miss you" retention offer (one free PT session) OR respectfully let them lapse. Dead members drain comms budget and skew metrics. Tightening the roster cuts operational drag.

• **Track the 10% in real time** — Set a simple spreadsheet with 5 categories (utilities, supplies, staffing, services, other). Hit 2% per category. Weekly check-ins. This isn't set-and

# Monthly Finance KPI Update — Execution Plan

• **Set a recurring calendar block for the 3rd working day of each month (9am, 90 mins max).** This removes decision fatigue on *when* to do it. Grace should pull last month's actuals by the 2nd, so data's ready.

• **Track only 5 KPIs, not 10.** For a gym: (1) Member revenue (memberships + classes), (2) Operating costs as % of revenue, (3) New member acquisition cost, (4) Member churn rate, (5) Cash position. Anything else dilutes focus.

• **Create a single one-page template in Google Sheets with red/yellow/green flags pre-built.** E.g., if member churn hits 8%+ it turns red. Grace sees trends instantly without analysis paralysis—just fixes what's broken.

• **Link directly to action:** Every KPI gets a 1-sentence note on what changed month-on-month and what she'll adjust *next month* (staffing, pricing, marketing spend). This forces accountability and kills data that just sits there.

• **Delegate the data collection to one staff member (likely manager or finance-minded person) by the 2nd of each month.** Grace reviews and interprets only. Saves her 60% of the time.

**Done right, this becomes a 30-minute review, not a frustrating admin task.**

# Financial Forecasting Execution for The Forge

**1. Pull last 12 months of actual data THIS WEEK**
Get your bank statements, membership revenue (broken down by tier), class pack sales, personal training, and operating costs into a spreadsheet. Don't estimate—use real numbers. This takes 2 hours max and is your foundation.

**2. Segment revenue by driver, not just total**
Model these separately: recurring membership (most predictable), class packs, PT sessions, retail/supplements. Each has different growth trajectories. Membership might grow 8% annually; PT could spike 30% if you hire staff. Your forecast is useless if it's averaged together.

**3. Build a 3-scenario model (not one forecast)**
Conservative (0% growth), base case (realistic based on current sales pipeline), aggressive (if you execute 2-3 growth initiatives). Show Grace what happens to cash flow and breakeven in each scenario—this drives actual decision-making, not pretty spreadsheets.

**4. Set the metric that matters: monthly cash position**
Forecast 12 months of *cash in/out*, not just profit & loss. If you're reinvesting in equipment or marketing, profit looks good but cash runs dry. This is what keeps her up at night—solve for that.

**5. Update it monthly (90 minutes max)**
Build it so Grace can plug in last month's actuals in under an hour, compare to forecast, and spot variances immediately. If reality diverges 15%+, that signals a problem needing a strategy tweak.

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# P&L Mapping for The Forge — Execution Plan

**1. Start with revenue buckets TODAY (don't wait for perfect data)**
Pull your last 3 months of invoices/till records and categorize every pound into: Group Classes | Personal Training | Add-ons (nutrition plans, merchandise, guest passes). Use a basic spreadsheet with columns: Revenue | Direct Costs (instructor pay, equipment wear) | Contribution Margin %. You'll spot gaps immediately—this is actionable rough data, not perfect accounting.

**2. Assign costs ruthlessly (this is where the insight lives)**
Don't split rent evenly across all services. For PT sessions: count actual trainer pay + any premium facility costs (if you block studio time). For classes: instructor fee + music/streaming + small equipment replacement. For add-ons: just COGS (cost of goods). The hard truth emerges when you see PT might be 60% margin vs. classes at 35%—that drives real decisions.

**3. Run a quick "contribution per hour" analysis**
Calculate revenue per trainer hour (PT) vs. per class slot (group classes). A PT session might generate £60 contribution in 60 mins; a class might be £150 in 45 mins across 15 people. This shows you where to push member acquisition effort—group classes scale better, but PT has higher per-unit profit.

**4. Test ONE add-on pricing immediately**
You likely undercharge or miscount add-ons. Pick one (nutrition audit, resistance bands, guest passes). Calculate actual cost + 70% markup minimum. Trial it for 2 weeks, track uptake. This tiny P&L line can become meaningful margin with

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Audit & Fix Your Automation Stack

• **Run a parallel test TODAY**: Pick 5 test email addresses, submit them through your website inquiry form, and track what they receive over 7 days. You'll instantly spot duplicates (same welcome email hitting twice) and broken sequences (trial offer never arrives). This takes 30 mins and beats theorizing.

• **Map your actual flow on paper**: List every platform touching enquiries (website form → email → booking system → SMS → gym software). Duplicates live in the *handoffs* — usually a Zapier rule + a platform automation both firing. Screenshot each tool's automation rules and highlight which ones touch the same person.

• **Kill the obvious culprits first**: If you're using both Mailchimp automations AND Zapier to welcome new leads, one's redundant. Pick the platform that integrates best with your booking system (probably your gym software) and delete the duplicate in the other. Test again.

• **Confirm the trial conversion step**: Your biggest leak is likely here — women book a trial but never receive pre-arrival details, cancellation confirmation, or follow-up after no-show. Check if your booking system sends confirmations *and* your email tool is also sending them. Keep only one.

• **Set a 30-day check-in**: After fixing, monitor your enquiry-to-trial conversion rate for two weeks. If it jumps 5-10%, the cleanup worked. If flat, something else is broken.

What's your booking system? (ClassPass, Mindbody, custom software?)

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge — Concrete Action Plan

**Identify your 3 quick wins:**
- **Member onboarding emails** (welcome sequence, class schedule, facility tour reminders) — template-heavy, sent 50+ times/month
- **Social media captions** (class highlights, member spotlights, weekly schedule posts) — repetitive tone, same angles reused
- **Cancellation/pause reason collection** (automated follow-up to departing members) — critical intel you're likely losing now

**Build these (not complex):**
- Use **Zapier + ChatGPT** or **Make.com** (free-tier sufficient). Set up: trigger → template prompt → output to email/scheduling tool
- For **onboarding**: Create one master prompt ("Generate a warm, 150-word welcome email mentioning class types: [list]. Member name: [variable]"). Test with 5 members first.
- For **social captions**: One prompt pulls last week's class data, generates 3 caption options weekly. You pick the best one in 2 minutes instead of 20.

**Reality check & timeline:**
- Week 1: Pick tool, write prompts (2–3 hours max)
- Week 2: Test live with 10% of members, measure open rates + response rates
- Week 3: Full rollout, track time saved

**Why this matters for Grace**: You reclaim 5–7 hours/month immediately. More importantly, those touchpoints stay *consistent* (higher retention signal) while you focus on member experience & gym operations that actually move revenue.

What's your biggest bottleneck right now — member communication, admin time, or member

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity Execution for The Forge Website

• **Get an SSL certificate TODAY (15 mins, £0-50/year)** — Non-negotiable first move. Use Let's Encrypt (free) through your hosting provider or buy from GoDaddy. This stops payment data being intercepted and kills Google's "not secure" warning that kills conversions.

• **Run Wordfence Security scan (free plugin, 10 mins)** — If you're on WordPress, install it now. It'll flag existing vulnerabilities, show you what's actually broken, and handle most threats automatically. Don't overthink a full audit yet.

• **Password-lock admin access & create a strong backup routine (20 mins)** — Change your WordPress login URL from `/wp-admin` (hackers hammer this), force strong passwords for all staff accounts, and set automatic daily backups. A hack that forces you offline costs more than prevention ever will.

• **Add a simple privacy policy + stripe/payment processor compliance (30 mins)** — Women will hesitate signing up if there's no clear data policy. Use a template generator (Termly/Iubenda) — you're not a lawyer, so don't pretend. This covers your liability when collecting payment info.

• **Outsource the heavy lifting after these wins** — Once these basics are locked, hire a freelance WP security specialist (£200-400) for a deeper audit. You'll know what questions to ask by then, and you won't waste money on stuff you don't need.

**Priority order: SSL → Wordfence → Passwords → Privacy policy.** Do these this week.

marketing.advice:
# The Forge — June Ad Performance Analysis

## Insights

**Lead quality is collapsing while cost-per-lead is tripling.** You've gone from £6/lead in April to £20/lead in June—a 233% increase—while close rate has halved from 29% (20/69) to 48% (10/21). The 48% close rate masks the real damage: you're generating far fewer qualified prospects. Your audience is either saturated, your creative has fatigued, or you're targeting people further down the consideration funnel.

**Your June spend cut by £105 versus May, but lead volume collapsed by 45%.** This wasn't a budget efficiency win—it looks like you pulled back spend mid-month or paused underperforming segments, which tanked volume without solving the underlying cost problem. June's profit (£1,538) is 33% lower than April's implied profit (~£2,310), despite similar spend.

**The April-to-May trend tells the real story.** Cost-per-lead more than doubled (£6→£14) even as spend increased 20%, and close rate stayed flat (29%→61%, actually improved). That's classic audience saturation or creative wear-out. June's data shows you're still advertising to a tired audience—you haven't refreshed creative or expanded targeting since the problem started in May.

---

## Actions

**1. Audit and refresh creative immediately.** Pull performance data by ad creative within June campaigns. Identify your top 3-5 performers from April (when CPL was £6) and check if they're still running in June. If they're still active, they're exhausted—pause them. Build 4-5 new video/image creative angles: testimonials from recent trial-to-member conversions, before/after transformations, "day in the life" at The Forge (community focus—women-only angle), pain-point messaging (time constraints, intimidation at mixed gyms), and a direct offer frame ("6 weeks free, zero commitment"). A/B test within the same audience segment to isolate which new creative moves the needle.

**2. Expand and segment your audience—stop relying on one core demographic.** Your saturation in May
